title: SEGMENT 15 — Bioprinted skin: from burn replacement to drug testing

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — Bioprinted skin: from burn replacement to drug testing
One application area where bioprinting has made faster clinical progress is skin.
Skin is, of all the major organs, the most amenable to bioprinting for several reasons.
It's relatively thin — a few hundred micrometers for epidermis, a couple of millimeters for full-thickness — so the vascularization wall is less of an obstacle.
It has a simple layered architecture: a basement membrane, a few cell types, a stratified epidermis on top.
It's accessible — you print it onto a wound, no internal surgery required.
And the clinical demand is enormous — burn patients, chronic wound patients, ulcers in diabetes — driving real investment in the technology.
Several companies have developed bioprinted skin products at various stages of clinical use.
CELLINK — now part of BICO Group — develops bioprinters and bio-inks widely used in skin tissue research.
Aspect Biosystems has developed microfluidic bioprinting platforms that can build complex multi-layer skin constructs.

The Wake Forest Institute, where Atala works, demonstrated mobile bioprinting systems for printing skin directly onto wounds — a system that could in principle be deployed in burn units and printed in situ rather than printed in a lab and applied as a sheet.
The basic recipe for bioprinted skin is a multi-layer construct.
The bottom layer is a dermal equivalent — a hydrogel, often collagen-based, embedded with fibroblasts, the connective tissue cells of the dermis.
The fibroblasts secrete collagen and other matrix proteins, gradually building up a dermal layer.
On top of the dermal equivalent, you print a layer of keratinocytes, the epithelial cells of the epidermis.
Over the course of culture or after implantation, the keratinocytes differentiate, stratify, and form a multilayer epidermis with a cornified outer layer.

More advanced versions of the recipe include melanocytes for pigmentation, vascular cells to start vessel networks in the dermis, immune cells to recreate skin immunology, and even hair follicle precursors in research-stage products.
The clinical applications are real and growing.

For burn patients, bioprinted skin offers an alternative to cultured epidermal autografts that includes proper dermal architecture — addressing one of the long-standing limitations of products like Epicel.
For chronic wounds — diabetic foot ulcers, pressure ulcers — bioprinted skin patches can accelerate healing and reduce amputations.

For drug and cosmetics testing, bioprinted skin constructs are increasingly replacing animal testing — the EU has banned cosmetics testing on animals, which has driven enormous demand for in vitro human skin models.
Bioprinted skin is, in many ways, where the field's promises are starting to come true.
It's not a printed liver.
It's not a printed kidney.
But it's a real product, used in real patients and real labs, that demonstrates the underlying technology works when the constraints are right.

The lessons from skin — the importance of multi-layer architecture, the role of co-culture with multiple cell types, the integration with host tissue after implantation, the value of in situ printing — all generalize to harder organs.
Skin is the proving ground.
